coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juan
Li, Huiyu
Wang, Long
Zhang, Ping
Zhou, Tianyu
Ding, Hong
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel, selective and eco-friendly sensor for the detection of tetracycline was developed by grafting imprinted polymers onto the surface of carbon quantum dots. A simple microwave-assisted approach was utilized to fabricate the fluorescent imprinted composites rapidly for the first time, which could shorten the polymerization time and simplify the experimental procedure dramatically. The novel composites not only demonstrated excellent fluorescence stability and special binding sites, but also could selectively accumulate target analytes. Under optimal conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of the composites decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of tetracycline from 20nM to 14µM. The detection limit of tetracycline was 5.48nM. The precision and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were also acceptable. Significantly, the practicality of this ultrasensitive sensor for tetracycline detection in milk was further validated, revealing the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, selectivity and low cost. This approach combines the high selective adsorption property of molecular imprinted polymers and the sensitivity of fluorescence detection. It is envisioned that the development of fluorescent molecularly imprinted composites will offer a new way of thinking for rapid analysis in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Microwave irradiation
Fluorescence detection
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs composites
Characterization of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs, inset: HRTEM image of CDs, (B) TEM image of the CDs@MIPs, and (C) SEM image of the CDs@MIPs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs (a) and CDs@MIPs (b). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a), CDs@NIPs (b), CDs@MIPs before (c) and after (d) template molecules extraction, CDs@MIPs with addition of 12µM Tc (e). The concentration of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs was 2.5mgmL−1.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@MIPs (A) and CDs@NIPs (B) with addition of the indicated concentrations of Tc in PBS buffer (10mM, pH=9.5); Stern–Volmer plots (C) from CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs with Tc. Error bars= ±standard deviation. The concentration of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs was 2.5mgmL− 1.

Selective adsorption of Tc, CTc, OTc, CAP, HQ, SMM, Trim, Cef and FF by CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs at pH 9.5 (PBS buffer, 10mM) (A). Influence of coexisting common ions (10µM) (B) and other molecules (5µM) (C) on the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs in the presence of Tc (5µM). The concentration of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs was 2.5mgmL−1.
Illustration of the preparation of CDs@MIPs.
The comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods of Tc fluorescence detection in recent years.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel, selective and eco-friendly sensor for the detection of tetracycline was developed by grafting imprinted polymers onto the surface of carbon quantum dots. A simple microwave-assisted approach was utilized to fabricate the fluorescent imprinted composites rapidly for the first time, which could shorten the polymerization time and simplify the experimental procedure dramatically. The novel composites not only demonstrated excellent fluorescence stability and special binding sites, but also could selectively accumulate target analytes. Under optimal conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of the composites decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of tetracycline from 20nM to 14µM. The detection limit of tetracycline was 5.48nM. The precision and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were also acceptable. Significantly, the practicality of this ultrasensitive sensor for tetracycline detection in milk was further validated, revealing the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, selectivity and low cost. This approach combines the high selective adsorption property of molecular imprinted polymers and the sensitivity of fluorescence detection. It is envisioned that the development of fluorescent molecularly imprinted composites will offer a new way of thinking for rapid analysis in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (Tc), as a member of broad spectrum antibiotics, has been extensively used in animal husbandry for the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections or as a feed additive to promote the growth of livestock [1–4]. However, excessive Tc, as well as the improper disposal of residues, can cause accumulation of antibiotics in animal products, including meat, milk and honey. The residues may potentially result in a risk of drug-resistant microorganisms spread and pose a serious threat to human health [1,3]. Increasing presence of Tc residues in food products has raised global concerns [5]. Both the European Union and the US Food and Drug Administration have established the maximum residue limit of Tc in milk (225nM and 900nM, respectively) [6,7]. Thus, it is of great necessity to develop a facile, rapid and reliable quantification methods for trace Tc residues in food products.

Up to now, various analytical methods for the detection of Tc have been developed, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], LC with tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [3,4], enzyme-linked aptamer assay [10,11] and colorimetric analysis [12,13], etc. Generally, HPLC/LC–MS technique shows high sensitivity and identification ability for target analytes, but the requirement of complicated operation procedures and expert technical skill limits their application in routine analysis. Enzyme-linked aptamer assay and colorimetric analysis are also not suitable due to the drawbacks of high cost and potential cross-reactivity. Recently, fluorescent detection has become a powerful approach in analytical fields, owing to its advantages of operational convenience, low cost, real-time detection and high sensitivity [14–16].

During the past decades, a series of fluorescent sensors have been designed to detect non-fluorescent Tc, such as proteins [17], Eu3+-based nanoparticles [18,19], metal–organic coordination polymers [20,21], CdTe quantum dots [22]. However, the reported sensors still lacked good anti-interference ability in complex samples, leading to false-positive test results. Meanwhile, new concerns have been raised over the potential toxicity and environmental hazards caused by heavy metals.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) have recently attracted considerable attention due to their advantages over conventional semiconductor quantum dots, such as easy synthesis, bright fluorescence, good stability, low cytotoxicity and convenient surface functionalization [23–26]. As a consequence, fascinating CDs have been explored as fluorescent probes for environmental or biological detection.

In our previous work, we have proposed a simple and cost-effective approach for the large-scale preparation of CDs by microwave-assisted technique [27]. Although preliminary investigation for Tc determination has been studied, it is still a challenge to design selective and reliable chemical sensors without cross-reactivity and false positives in complex samples. Herein, as a continuation of our previous work, trace Tc in milk samples were selected as the analytical model to evaluate the advanced applications of CDs.

Molecularly imprinting technique has become an upcoming approach for the preparation of polymers with tailor-made receptor cavities of molecules and cells [28–30]. Several groups have prepared molecularly imprinted materials on the surface of quantum dots (such as Mn-doped ZnS QDs [28], CQDs [29], CdTe QDs [31], CdSe QDs [32], graphene quantum dots [33]). These optical sensors show selective recognition to the template molecules based on a “key and lock” model. Generally, the polymerization reaction was completed by conventional heating [28,29,33]. However, the requirement of long reaction time (15–24h) and the ability of poor penetration limited the practical applications. In this work, microwave-assisted preparation was proposed as an alternative route to replace the conventional heating method, which achieved significant promotion of reaction rate and products yield. To the best of our knowledge, preparation of CDs@MIPs by microwave irradiation has not been reported before.

In this work, a facile microwave-assisted route was designed for the rapid synthesis of CDs@MIPs. CDs were prepared according to our previous work and then modified with multi-aminosilane. MIPs were fabricated by a simple sol–gel hydrolysis and condensation reaction. Compared with conventional heating methods, the advantages of microwave irradiation were obvious, such as significant savings of reaction time and energy. The CDs@MIPs composites showed good selectivity, high extraction efficiency and more accessible sites for the absorption of Tc. Furthermore, the presented strategy has great potential applications for the preconcentration, recovering and direct determination of trace Tc in milk samples simultaneously without secondary pollution.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Citric acid, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Tetracycline (Tc) and other standards were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol, ethanol and other chemical reagents were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of various pH values were prepared with different ratios of NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, K2HPO4 and ultrapure water. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩ cm was obtained from the United States Milli-Q purification system.

The morphology and microstructure of CDs and MIPs/NIPs capped CDs (CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs) were characterized by a transmission election microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JEM-6700F). Samples for TEM were obtained by drying sample droplets from water dispersion onto a 300-mesh Cu grid coated with carbon films. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were recorded on a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp using right-angle geometry. In the whole experiments, a 1cm path-length quartz cuvette was used.

The CDs were synthesized from citric acid according to our previous work [27]. Briefly, citric acid (5g) and dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) (1g) were dissolved in 10mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. Afterwards, the mixture was sonicated for 2min and then heated in an ordinary household microwave oven at 750W for 2min 30s. After natural cooling to room temperature, the powders were purified by precipitation with ethanol several times and separated by centrifugation. Purified CDs were dispersed in 120mL ethanol. All the starting materials used for the CDs synthesis are non-toxic and environmentally friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, APTS (1.2mL) was added into 16mL CDs ethanol solution. The mixture was stirred for 30min and heated in an ordinary household microwave oven at 375W for 4min. After natural cooling to room temperature, the APTS-modified CDs (CDs–APTS) were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether twice, and then dispersed in 40mL anhydrous ethanol for further experiments.

The MIPs were fabricated on the silica-coated CDs via a typical sol–gel polymerization. In a 50mL beaker, 100mg Tc (template) was added to 12mL CDs–APTS ethanol solution under stirring. Then, 1.5mL of APTS and 1.2mL TEOS were injected, respectively. Subsequently, 1.5mL of 6% NH3·H2O were added to the reaction beaker and the mixture was stirred for 30min. Finally, the resulting solution was transferred to a microwave digestion tank and heated in the high-pressure microwave oven. Microwave irradiation was carried out with a programmed temperature control as follows: initial from room temperature to 40°C within 5min, from 40°C to 60°C within 3min and finally keeping at 60°C for 1h.

The final products were collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with anhydrous ethanol, ultrapure water and methanol under ultrasonic agitation, respectively. The original Tc template molecules in the composites were extracted with methanol and 10% acidified methanol, until no Tc could be detected in the washing solvent by high performance liquid chromatography. The composites were dried at 60°C overnight to eliminate the solvent completely. Finally, the CDs@MIPs were dispersed in 40mL water and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C prior to use.
As a control, the non-imprinted polymers capped CDs (CDs@NIPs) were synthesized in the same manner without the addition of template molecules (Tc).

The recognition capacity of CDs@MIPs was measured with different Tc concentrations by fluorescence spectroscopy, and the fluorescent intensity of the maximum emission peak was used for quantitative analysis. The fluorescence measurements were carried out by fitting the excitation wavelength at 355nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10nm.

The stock solution of CDs@MIPs was diluted with PBS buffer solution (pH=9.5). Then, 100µL above mixture and different amounts of Tc standard solution were sequentially injected into the cuvette. The final concentration of Tc can be known through a simple calculation. All the solutions were shaken thoroughly before measurement. The controlled experiments of CDs@NIPs were the same as those of CDs@MIPs.

All of the milk samples were purchased from local supermarkets and pretreated with trichloroacetic acid to remove the proteins according to the procedures reported previously with a slight modification [19]. Milk samples and 1% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid solution were added together into a centrifuge tube, fully mixed and then sonicated for 15min. After centrifuging at 10,000rpm for 8min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22µm membrane to eliminate particulate matters. The pH was adjusted to neutrality with NaOH and the filtrate was stored at 4°C away from light before fluorescent measurements.
Scheme 1 illustrates the major synthetic steps of CDs@MIPs (see Section 2 for details, ESI†). In this work, we focused on the application of microwave irradiation in the preparation procedures.

The CDs were directly prepared within 2min 30s by microwave in one pot according to our previous work [27]. Prior to polymerization, APTS, as the precursor of silica layer, was chemically modified on the surface of CDs by microwave heating within 4min. Silica is an ideal coating for protecting CDs and enhancing the reaction between CDs and imprinted polymers [29,32]. Next, the CDs@MIPs were prepared via a simple sol–gel polymerization in the presence of CDs–APTS as fluorescent materials, Tc as template, APTS as functional monomer, TEOS as crosslinker and NH3·H2O as catalyst. Microwave heating technique was firstly introduced as an efficient and simple manner to coat CDs and initiate the polymerization [34]. Compared to conventional heating, significant promotion of the reaction rate was observed [28,29]. The polymerization could be accomplished within only 1h in our work.

Although the final composites were obtained by multi-steps, microwave irradiation could provide intensive, efficient and homogeneous energy and as a consequence significantly shorten the reaction time. It is concluded that microwave-assisted technique is more feasible and promising in the rapid and efficient preparation of CDs@MIPs composites. In addition, the raw materials are green and low cost, avoiding complicated post-treatment and the use of organic reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A shows the TEM images of as-prepared CDs from citric acid at different magnifications, revealing that they consist of mono-dispersed nanodots with an average diameter of 5nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.348nm, which may be attributed to the (002) facet of graphite (in accordance with other literatures) [35,36]. During fast and efficient microwave irradiation, the homogeneous solution may undergo thermal carbonization and pyrolysis of citric acid with the formation of gas, leading to a uniform nucleation growth [27]. The morphology of CDs@MIPs was characterized by TEM and SEM (Fig. 1B and C). The particles exhibited relatively narrow size distributions with around ca. 55nm diameters. However, differences in the morphologies of the resulting CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs were observed. Tc molecules competed with the electron-donating monomers (APTS) for the adsorption onto the surface of CDs, which impeded the chain-growth polymerization in the CDs@MIPs. Thus, spherical nanostructure could be observed in the CDs@MIPs composites. By contrast, CDs@NIPs, without Tc added, were mostly wrapped in the polymer networks and tended to form the silica gel bulk (Fig. S1, ESI†).

FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs@MIPs were collected and compared ( Fig. 2A). The most characteristic peaks of CDs are the absorption bands of carboxyl C=O stretching at 1710cm−1 and the broad adsorption of –OH groups between 3000cm− 1 and 3500cm− 1 (curve a) [27]. As shown in the FT-IR absorption spectrum of CD@MIPs, the strong and broad peaks around 1062cm−1 and 1113cm− 1 (curve b) are assigned to the Si–O–C and Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching, respectively [32], which is not found in the spectrum of CDs (curve a). Peaks at about 792cm−1 and 460cm−1 (curve b) also suggest the existence of the Si–O vibrations. Additionally, the broad absorption peak around 3434cm−1 (N–H bond) and 2933cm− 1 (C–H stretching band) are attributed to the aminopropyl groups [33]. The peak at 1689cm−1 (secondary amide C=O stretching in curve b) instead of the peak at 1710cm− 1 (carboxyl C=O stretching in curve a) demonstrates the presence of amide bonding (–CO–NH–) [29,33]. All the above bands further confirm that the imprinted polymers, generated from sol–gel condensation of APTS and TEOS, have been successfully grafted on the surface of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2B, CDs show a symmetric and strong fluorescence emission at 440nm when excited at 350nm, whereas the as-prepared CDs@MIPs show a symmetric fluorescence emission at 450nm when excited at 355nm. Compared with CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs demonstrate distinct differences in both emission positions and fluorescent intensities. As is well known to us, a single charge (Si–O-groups and hydroxyl ions) on the surface can generate an electric field, which may lead to the recombination of trapped charge carriers and require dangling bonds at the particle surface [31,33]. Therefore, significant quenching and a red-shift of fluorescence emission were observed.

Then, we investigated the stability of the CDs@MIPs. There were no discernible changes in both fluorescent intensity and peak characteristics within 60min excitation at 365nm with a UV beam (Fig. S2, ESI†), as confirmed by observed 2.4% RSD (relative standard deviation) for 13 repeated detections. Besides, the products were stored at 4°C and could be stable for more than two weeks. After two weeks, the intensity retained 94% of its initial response.

The pH effect on the fluorescent intensity of the CDs@MIPs was studied by tuning pH from 1.0 to 13.0. As shown in Fig. S3A (ESI†), the fluorescent intensity is relatively stable in the interval 4.0–11.0. However, the fluorescent intensity decreases dramatically in strong acidic and basic media. Such pH-dependent fluorescence behavior is probably due to the dissociation of silica shell on the surface. At low pH, the surface of the composites may be dissolved, which results in the changes of surface defects and the quenching of fluorescence. With increasing pH from 11.0 to 13.0, the fluorescent intensity shows a drastic decrease, because the silica shell may be ionized and the OH− could nucleophilically attack the surface defects [32].

The eco-friendly fluorescent CDs@MIPs can be applied to the Tc sensing. As shown in Fig. 2B, after templates removal, the fluorescent intensity of CDs@MIPs (curve d) was restored nearly to that of CDs@NIPs (curve b). Furthermore, the fluorescent intensity of CDs@MIPs could be quenched efficiently with the addition of Tc (curve e). The MIPs could selectively absorb Tc molecules onto the surface of CDs. Due to the overlap of the absorption band of Tc (~358nm) and the excitation band of CDs (~354nm) (Fig. S4, ESI†), the excitation energy could transfer from CDs (donor) to Tc (acceptor), which led to deficient excitation and a highly efficient quenching performance [31,37]. Based on the fluorescence resonant energy transfer system, a rapid and sensitive sensing route for Tc was thus proposed.

After exposing CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs to the Tc solution (12μmolL−1), the ratios of the observed fluorescent intensities (I) to the initial fluorescent intensities (I0) were plotted against the response time (Fig. S5, ESI†). Tc coordination with CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs reached equilibrium at approximately 10min and 14min, respectively, and then remained constant. Note that, CDs@NIPs had larger polymer networks and silica gel bulk, which might block Tc diffusion paths to the binding sites through cavity channels (as evidence in Fig. 1, TEM images) [31]. Therefore, for CDs@NIPs, the penetration of Tc into the polymers was more difficult and the change of fluorescent intensity was observed later than that of CDs@MIPs.

The pH value affected not only the surface charge of CDs@MIPs, but also the interaction between Tc and CDs@MIPs, thereby impacting the quenching performance. We investigated the pH effect on the fluorescence-quenched efficiency (Effq ) by turning pH from 4.5 to 10.5. Effq was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
E f
f
q
( % )
= ( F
0
- F ) / F
0
where F and F 0 represented the fluorescent intensities of CDs@MIPs in the presence and absence of Tc (12µmolL− 1), respectively. As shown in Fig. S3B (ESI†), the Effq of this sensor increases with increasing the pH from 4.5 to 9.5, and then decreases slightly from 9.5 to 10.5. Thus, pH 9.5 was chosen as the optimum pH for the Tc determination, which could be ascribed to the electrostatic attraction between Tc and the APTS amino (pKa=9.7) in CDs@MIPs.

Under the optimum experimental conditions established, the calibration plots were generated. The fluorescent intensities of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs were measured after equilibrium in PBS buffer solution (pH=9.5) and plotted against the Tc concentrations ranging from 0 to 14µmolL− 1 ( Fig. 3). Although both CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs showed fluorescence response to Tc, the fluorescent intensity of CDs@MIPs was quenched notably with increasing the concentration of Tc (Fig. 3A). However, the non-imprinted composites CDs@NIPs did not exhibit obvious difference with the addition of Tc (Fig. 3B). CDs@MIPs turned out to be more sensitive for the Tc determination. The fluorescence quenching performance can be described by the Stern–Volmer equation:
(2)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
C
where F 0 and F represent the fluorescent intensities at a given Tc concentration and in a Tc free solution, respectively, C is the concentration of Tc, and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of the target molecule [38]. As shown in Fig. 3C, the Stern–Volmer plots of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs both exhibited linear relationship by changing the concentration of Tc from 20nM to 14µM. The regression equations were:
(3)
CDs @ MIPs :
F 0
/ F = 1 + 0 . 0916
C
T c
, µ mol
L
− 1
,
(
R
2
= 0 . 9972 )
(4)
CDs @ NIPs :
F 0
/ F = 1 + 0.0317
C
T c
, µ mol
L
− 1
,
(
R
2
= 0.9934 )

The imprinted factor (IF), which is used to evaluate the specific recognition of the composites [28], can be calculated by the following equation:
(5)
IF =
K
s v , M I P
/
K
s v , N I P
where Ksv,MIP and Ksv,NIP are the linear slopes in Fig. 3C. Under optimum conditions, the IF was calculated to be 2.89, which indicated that strong interactions between Tc and the imprinted cavities created an efficient imprinting effect on the templates recognition.

Binding experiments were carried out to investigate the recognition and adsorption ability of the composites. Fig. S6 shows the binding isotherms of Tc on the CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs. With increasing the initial concentration of Tc, the adsorption capacity of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs increased rapidly. However, the amount of Tc bound to CDs@MIPs was higher than that bound to CDs@NIPs, which highlights the efficiency of molecularly imprinting.

Reproducibility is another important property for the application of sensors. The CDs@MIPs sensor could be reused for at least 6 repeated analyses, after subsequent elution of Tc molecules by methanol–acetic acid (90:10, v/v) for 10min. With the number of reuses increasing, the template molecules located at the interior area were difficult to remove due to the low diffusion rates in highly cross-linked polymer matrix. The results showed that the recovery of fluorescence decreased slightly after the sixth cycle, but was still more than 90%. Meanwhile, the morphology of the composites has no distinct difference, revealing the stable rigid polymeric matrix. The repeatability of one sensor was carried out for the detection of 30nM Tc, and the RSD was 2.4% (n=7). Also, the repeatability of the CDs@MIPs sensor was investigated with detections of 30nM Tc by using five freshly prepared sensors under the same conditions. The RSD was calculated to be 3.2%.

Therefore, the proposed sensor could be applied to the quantitative analysis of trace Tc. The linear range of the calibration curve was obtained from 0.02 to 14µmolL− 1. As shown in Fig. S7 (ESI†), the detection limit, calculated based on a 3σ/slope [39,40], was down to 5.48nmolL− 1, which is lower or at least comparable to the sensors reported in recent years [18–22]. Meanwhile, the precision and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were also acceptable.

Firstly, we tested the recognition ability of CDs@MIPs towards other tetracycline analogs, such as oxytetracycline (OTc) and chlorotetracycline (CTc) ( Fig. 4A). The chemical structures of Tc, OTc and CTc are shown in Fig. S8 (ESI†). Due to the very similar chemical structure, OTc and CTc can also get into the recognition sites and quench the fluorescence of the sensor. The IF values of OTc and CTc were nearly close to 2.7, indicating that the CDs@MIPs had a specific imprint affinity towards Tc analogs.

Subsequently, in order to illustrate the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs@MIPs towards Tcs, control experiments were carried out containing other non-specific compounds with similar structure, such as chloramphenicol (CAP), hydroquinone (HQ), sulfamonomethoxine (SMM), trimethoprim (Trim), ceftriaxone (Cef) and florfenicol (FF) (Fig. 4A). Obviously, there was no significant difference in the quenching efficiency of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs. Note that, compared with our previous work [27], the selectivity for Tc detection was improved significantly.

There usually exist some common metal ions, biological compounds, amino acids, vitamins and saccharides in milk samples. To address the question whether foreign coexisting substances show effect on the analysis of Tc or not, further interference experiments were performed in the presence of Tc together with other coexisting ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, PO43-, SO42−, Cl−) or compounds (glucose, sucrose, maltose, Val, Lys, Phe, Tyr, Pro, Glu, Ala, Cys, ascorbic acid and melamine). As shown in Fig. 4B and C, there is no obvious effect on the assaying Tc. Because of the specific spatial effect, other coexisting substances cannot be bound to the recognition sites, which proves the high selectivity for target analytes.

As a result, the proposed CDs@MIPs-based sensor exhibited more accessible sites for the absorption of Tc and high selectivity without cross-reactivity, which offers new developments in the practical applications of target compounds detection in complicated samples.

Up to now, the main problem in the detection of trace Tc in milk is their low concentration and the complexity of the co-existence. Our proposed method can selectively detect Tc in complex samples without interference. The LOD can satisfy the maximum residue limits of Tc in milk established by the European Union and the US Food and Drug Administration (225nM and 900nM, respectively). To validate the applicability of the sensor, the proposed method was employed to analyze Tc in real milk samples. As shown in Table S1, no Tc in the milk samples was determined. The results were consistent with those obtained by the classical method [41]. A recovery study was carried out on the milk samples spiked with different amounts of Tc. The concentrations of Tc in the spiked samples by the proposed method were in good agreement with those of Tc added, along with the quantitative recoveries ranging from 97.3% to 105.3% (Table S1). The determined values of the present study, together with recently reported data for Tc detection are listed in Table 1. The results indicated that the proposed method provided wide dynamic range, good accuracy and a lower detection limit. Therefore, the CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor is more suitable and promising for the on-site and rapid Tc analysis in complex samples.

In summary, a facile, rapid and cost-effective synthetic method was proposed by grafting imprinted recognition sites onto the surface of fluorescent CDs. Microwave heating technique was firstly introduced as an efficient route to prepare CDs@MIPs, which could shorten the reaction time dramatically. The combination of fluorescence detection and imprinting technique not only made the sensor applicable to detect Tc molecules directly, but also improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. The as-prepared CDs@MIPs composites were applied to the trace detection of Tc in milk samples successfully. This eco-friendly optical sensor demonstrated a lower detection limit and good accuracy in complex samples analysis, which reveals great potential in advanced analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (Grant no. 20150204070GX).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.08.024.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Commission Regulation (EU) No 37/2010, 22 December 2009. Offic. J. Eur. Union L 15/1.
The Code of Federal Regulations, Title 21, Part 556, Section 152, 500, and 720. US Government Printing Office, Washington, DC, 2003.
